title: The Canterbury Tales. The Merchant's Tale. by Geoffrey Chaucer — Sad Poetry Lines
content:
THE PROLOGUE.

"Weeping and wailing, care and other sorrow,
I have enough, on even and on morrow,"
Quoth the Merchant, "and so have other mo',
That wedded be; I trow that it be so;
For well I wot it fareth so by me.
I have a wife, the worste that may be,
meaning: "The Canterbury Tales. The Merchant's Tale." by Geoffrey Chaucer is a sad and love and deep and nature and inspirational poem consisting of 1240 lines. The poet explores themes of sad and love and deep and nature and inspirational through vivid imagery and emotional depth. Beginning with "THE PROLOGUE. ...", this piece captures the essence of human experience and invites reflection on the deeper currents of life and feeling. This work is part of Geoffrey Chaucer's celebrated body of poetry that continues to resonate with readers worldwide.
